w:r.w:t: Common foundation for all teams. Read

Design a solution that helps the Nordika Studio team integrate their catalog on 6 marketplaces with minimal manual effort. Your solution must handle messy data, translate content, map categories to each marketplace's taxonomy, validate the output, and give the seller clear control over what gets published.

The form of the solution is up to you — a web app, a conversational agent, a command-line tool with a rich feedback loop, a custom-trained model, a workflow of specialized agents, or anything in between. What matters is that a non-technical seller can understand, trust, and control what the system does.
Normalize inconsistent values if needed (casing, typos, boolean variants, separators), if necessary
Recover missing attributes by extracting information from titles, descriptions and specifications, if necessary
Translate titles, descriptions and localized attribute values into IT and DE.
Map each Nordika category to an Amazon
(same for all countries) and a Google Product Category (specific to each country).
Validate each product against the target schema (required fields, constraints, enums).
What the seller must be able to do

A black-box pipeline that silently produces 6 files is not enough. Whatever form your solution takes, the seller must be able to trigger, monitor, inspect, override, and learn from the integration process. Think about: transparency of decisions, handling of edge cases, bulk vs per-SKU control, human-in-the-loop for ambiguous cases, progress feedback, error recovery.
Every new product has to be integrated on 6 marketplaces, each with its own rules.
Titles and descriptions must be translated into Italian and German.
The internal catalog is noisy: missing values, typos, inconsistent casing.
Marketplace validation errors mean back-and-forth edits that cost hours per SKU.
200-product catalog from Nordika. 80 columns. Bilingual (EN/FR). Intentionally noisy — this is part of the challenge.
values (~250 entries, including the 52 detailed in
Attributes that apply to every Amazon
(title, brand, price, dimensions, etc.). Defines types, constraints, and which attributes must be localized.
. Contains 52 product_types. Your job is to pick the right ones. Most specific_attributes are
(they drive ranking and filter facets); only a handful are
Google Shopping feed attributes — types, constraints, requirement levels, localization flags, and TSV encoding rules.
Google Shopping product taxonomies, hierarchical, one per language (public Google reference files).
(final selling price in EUR, what the customer sees)
The machine-readable schema is split across
(specific_attributes per product_type) — use them if your agent needs to parse or validate attribute rules programmatically. The sections below summarize the key information for quick reading.
Each feed targets a single marketplace. The
field on every line determines the locale for all attributes flagged
in the YAML references. Mapping in
attribute (common + specific) is present and satisfies its constraints.
: 1 to 200 characters, no ALL CAPS, no promotional characters (
: 3 to 5 bullets, each 1 to 250 characters, sentence case.
Expected Amazon FR output (1 JSONL line, showing the common attributes only):
"Chaise de salle à manger en tissu bouclette crème pieds chêne naturel"
"Chaise de salle à manger contemporaine tapissée en tissu bouclette crème doux..."
"Tissu bouclette crème 100% polyester doux au toucher"
"Hauteur d'assise 46 cm adaptée aux tables standard"
— they drive ranking and filter facets rather than feed acceptance. The one exception is
, which is required because it is a primary category-browse facet. Completeness still matters (cf. §5.3), so fill every
you can derive from the source:
Esszimmerstuhl aus cremefarbenem Bouclé-Stoff mit Beinen aus natürlicher Eiche
Sedia da pranzo in tessuto bouclé crema con gambe in rovere naturale
— use it if your agent needs to parse or validate attribute rules programmatically. The tables below summarize the same information for quick reading.
Each feed targets a single locale declared in
in the YAML must be expressed in that language — including
Google taxonomy node (numeric ID or full localized path)
must be in the target-country language.
TSV encoding: no raw tabs inside values, line breaks escaped as literal
Example — SKU NKS-00042, Google FR row
Each product has an initial stock quantity in the
column of the source catalog. Use it as-is for the initial integration.
A small number of SKUs will have
— your agent must decide whether to publish them anyway (as
) or skip them entirely. Both are defensible; document the choice.
Stock management across marketplaces (allocation, restocking, sync) is the subject of the
Each product has a single price in the
column — this is the final selling price in EUR, the same on all marketplaces, for all countries. Publish it as-is. No margin computation, no VAT handling, no currency conversion to manage here.
Price optimization is the subject of the
. English is the safer pivot because the French text sometimes contains encoding artifacts and typos.
Required is a minimum, not a target ⚠️
Passing validation with only the required fields filled is
. Marketplaces rank and filter products based on how complete their listings are:
On Amazon, listings with rich attributes (
, etc.) appear in more filter facets and rank higher in relevance. A product with only the bare minimum is often invisible in category browse pages.
On Google Shopping, the recommended columns (
) directly affect ad eligibility and CTR. Products missing them show up less in shopping ads.
Bullet points and detailed descriptions drive organic conversions. "Valid but empty" listings convert poorly.
Rule of thumb for your agent

: fill every attribute you can reasonably derive from the source data — from the structured columns, from free text extraction, or from sensible defaults. Required fields are the floor; completeness is what sells.
Feed files — proof that the integration works

Your integration is considered successful when your solution can produce these 6 files. They are the tangible output of your pipeline, the thing a reviewer (or Nordika's founder) can open and inspect to verify that the plumbing is real.
Amazon FR feed, one product per line
a checkpoint, not a final deliverable

. Getting them right means your mapping, translation, normalization and validation logic all work end-to-end. Getting them wrong (missing fields, wrong language, broken enums) tells you exactly where your pipeline breaks.

writing a small auto-check that parses your feeds and verifies them against the YAML schemas (required fields present, enums valid, lengths within bounds, locale correct) is a cheap way to catch regressions while you iterate. Bonus if the seller can trigger it from your solution and get a pass/fail report per marketplace.
The full attribute requirements depend on the chosen
as a catch-all is technically valid but makes your listings poor. Picking a too-specific type may force requirements you cannot satisfy.
Bullet points don't exist in the source catalog. You must generate them from

Bonus — watch out for scale: a naive "one LLM call per SKU per marketplace" means 1,200 calls just for 200 products. Think about caching, batching, and which parts actually need an LLM vs simple rules.
